source_metadata.title_raw: Alexander Abbe, Esq. - A-19-081 - June 26, 2019 - Santa Clara

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 26, 2019

Alexander Abbe, Esq.
Assistant City Attorney
1500 Warburton Ave
Santa Clara, CA 95050

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-19-081

Dear Mr. Abbe:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act ("Act")¹ on behalf of City of Santa Clara Councilmember Karen Hardy
Councilmember Raj Chahal, and Mayor Lisa M. Gillmor. Please note that we are only providing
advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other conflict of interest
prohibitions such as the common law or Section 1090.

        Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1.      Does the Act prohibit Councilmembers Karen Hardy and Raj Chahal from participating in
discussions and decisions related to the proposed specific plan for the El Camino Real focus area
and an associated circulation plan, given that their residences are less than 500 feet of the focus
area?

2.      Does the Act prohibit Mayor Lisa Gillmor from participating in discussions and decisions
related to the proposed El Camino Real specific plan and an associated circulation plan, given her
ownership of a real estate company, Gillmor & Associates, which owns two properties within 500
feet of the focus area?

CONCLUSION

1-2.    No. Even if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material effect on the
Councilmembers' residences and Mayor Gillmor's business properties, they may take part in the

_______________
        ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 2

decisions under the public generally exception because the decisions will affect at least 25 percent
of the businesses in the jurisdiction and will not uniquely affect either the Councilmembers'
residences or the Mayor's properties.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of Santa Clara is drafting a specific plan for the El Camino Real focus area. El
Camino Real is the City's main east-west thoroughfare and its most visible and identifiable
commercial corridor. The vision for El Camino Real in the City's general plan is to transform the
focus area from a series of automobile-oriented strip malls to a tree-lined, pedestrian, and transit-
oriented corridor with a mix of residential and retail uses.

The proposed El Camino Real focus area would include the properties fronting El Camino
Real, as well as some additional properties up to two blocks away from the roadway. Most of the
properties in the focus area currently have commercial or mixed-use zoning. The objectives for
preparation of the specific plan are to establish a land use plan, urban design standards, and a policy
framework that will guide future development and provide developers and the community with
clear direction regarding form, massing, density, and streetscape for new development.

In addition to the development standards for properties adjacent to El Camino Real, the City
will consider adopting a circulation plan that would potentially make changes to the roadway itself.
The proposed changes include the removal of travel lanes, the widening of the center median, the
addition of bulb-outs, adding buffered bicycle lanes, and the removal of on-street parking. The
Community Development Department detailed five different proposals in the circulation plan:

• Minor modifications: widen median, narrow traffic lanes, add landscaping, remove pork
chop islands at intersections.
• Remove on-street parking to accommodate buffered bike lane with bus boarding island.
• Remove on-street parking to accommodate cycle track with bus bulb-out.
• Roadway reallocation: remove a travel lane(s), widen center median and sidewalks, add
bulb-outs.
• Roadway reallocation: remove a travel lane(s), keep parking, add parking-protected bike
lane, widen sidewalks, add bulb-outs.

Councilmember Karen Hardy:

Councilmember Hardy and her husband own their primary residence, which is immediately
adjacent to the border of the proposed El Camino Real focus area. Their residence is less than 500
feet from the focus area, subject to an impending land use plan, urban design standards, and a
policy framework for future development.

Councilmember Raj Chahal:

Councilmember Chahal and his wife own their primary residence, which is less than 500
feet from the focus area. Two of the five proposed options of the circulation plan involve the
removal of travel lanes, potentially altering traffic levels near the Councilmember's house.

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 3

Mayor Lisa Gillmor:

Mayor Lisa Gillmor was elected at-large to represent the entire City in 2018. Mayor Gillmor's home is more than 7,000 feet away from the focus area. In addition to owning her residence, Mayor Gillmor owns more than 10 percent of the business, Gillmor & Associates. The company is a close corporation owned by the Mayor and members of her family.

Gillmor & Associates is a real estate and property management firm, which focuses on residential sales, commercial sales and leasing, land sales and leasing, and multi-family rentals, and owns nine properties within the City limits. Of those nine properties, two parcels are within 500 feet of the boundaries of the focus area. Of the 778 commercial parcels Citywide, 299 of the parcels are within 500 feet of the focus area. Additionally, Gillmor & Associates owns and operates seven properties that are more than 1,500 feet away from the focus area.

Future development projects that would be subject to the El Camino Real specific plan have the potential to increase the inventory of residential and commercial properties in the City. This, in turn, may increase the need for the type of services that Gillmor & Associates provides, and may result in additional business for Gillmor & Associates or for its competitors.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes, participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests.

Section 87103(b) indicates that an official has a financial interest in "[a]ny real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." Section 87103(d) states that an official has a financial interest in "[a]ny business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management." Lastly, Section 87103 state that a public official also has an interest in his or her "personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family, also known as the 'personal financial effects' rule."2 We now consider the financial effect of the decisions at issue on the officials' respective interests.

Foreseeability:

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a.) Under the facts you have provided, the officials' properties and businesses are not explicitly involved in the decision. Thus, as applied to each officials' respective real property interest, Regulation 18701(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."

2 Effects on an official's personal finances are not considered separately from the effect on an official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Thus, we do not consider the personal financial effects rule further.

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 4

Materiality – Real Property:

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. As relevant to the facts concerning
Councilmembers Hardy and Chahal, Regulation 18702.2 provides that the financial effect of the
decisions on the officials' respective real property interest is material if the decision:

• Involves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is
clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the
official's property (Regulation 18702.2(a)(7).)

Councilmember Karen Hardy:

Councilmember Hardy and her husband own their primary residence. Their residence is
immediately adjacent to the border of the proposed El Camino Real focus area. Considering that the
Councilmember's home is less than 500 feet from the focus area, subject to a land use plan, urban
design standards, and a policy framework for future development, the impact of specific plan -
related decisions on the official's property is presumed to be material. You have not presented clear
and convincing evidence to rebut the presumption. Accordingly, Councilmember Hardy has a
potentially disqualifying conflict of interest unless the public generally exception discussed below
applies.

Councilmember Raj Chahal:

Councilmember Chahal and his wife own their primary residence, which is less than 500
feet from the focus area. You indicated that a couple of the circulation plan options have the
potential to substantially alter traffic levels near the Councilmember's house. Moreover, you have
not presented clear and convincing evidence that any specific plan-related decisions will not
measurably impact the Councilmember's property. As such, Councilmember Chahal also has a
potentially disqualifying conflict of interest unless the public generally exception applies.

Materiality – Business Entity:

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides the materiality standard for an interest in a business entity,
including a source of income, not explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18702.3.) Under
Regulation 18702.1(b), a financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information
would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a
change in the value of a privately-held business entity, such as a decision that:

(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects,
traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or
permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives

Mayor Lisa Gillmor:

You indicated that future development projects within the proposed specific plan have the
potential to increase the inventory of residential and commercial properties, potentially increasing

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 5

the need for Gillmor & Associates' services. Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable that specific
plan-related decisions will have a material financial effect on the Mayor's business.

Public Generally:

Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the
decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the general rule:

A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a
significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is
not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment.

To invoke the exception, the official must establish that a significant segment of the public
is affected and the effect on his or her interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant
segment. For purposes of the exception, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25
percent of (1) the businesses or nonprofit entities; (2) the real property, commercial real property, or
residential real property; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation
18703(b).)

You provided that 34.8 percent of Citywide commercial parcels are within 500 feet of the
focus area, as the thoroughfare is the City's most visible and identifiable corridor. As such, the
decision would affect a significant segment of the public. Any effects on the Councilmembers'
residences, as well as the Mayor's business properties, near El Camino Real appear to be common
amongst the thousands of commercial and residential properties located nearby. While some
businesses may lose parking, or pedestrian access points, these effects are also mitigated by the
addition of other parking areas or points of entry. It appears that neither the Councilmembers'
residences nor the Mayor's properties will experience any unique or disproportionate effects.
Accordingly, Councilmembers Hardy and Chahal and Mayor Gillmor may take part in the El
Camino Real specific plan decisions at issue under the Act.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Ryan O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division
